10.1016/j.jmp.2008.01.001
Standard theories of extensive measurement assume that the objects to be measured form a complete order, with respect to the relevant property. 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         
                            Roberts and Luce (1968) , give an axiomatization that does not assume completeness. They assume, however, that all objects are “conditionally connected”, in the sense that if  
                               a 
                               ≿ 
                               b 
                             &  
                               a 
                               ≿ 
                               c 
                            , then  
                               b 
                               ≿ 
                               c 
                             or  
                               c 
                               ≿ 
                               b 
                            . One of the theories presented by  Holman (1974) , does not require any order relation. Nevertheless, his theory does not allow for incomparability, in the sense defined below. 
                       In this paper, representation and uniqueness theorems will be presented for a theory that departs radically from this completeness assumption. We shall suppose only that the objects of measurement form a quasi-order, 
                         2 
                      
                      
                         2 
                         See the  Appendix  for definitions of types of orders and their properties. 
                       as regards the property in question. This allows for a strong form of incomparability between objects.

In Section  2 , we will show that any quasi-order on a countable set can be represented by vectors of real numbers. In Sections  3 and 4 , which constitute the main part of the paper, it is shown that if such an order is supplemented by a concatenation operator, yielding a relational structure that satisfies a set of axioms similar to the standard axioms for an extensive structure, we obtain a scale possessing the crucial properties of a ratio scale. Incomparability is thus compatible with extensive measurement. Finally, Section  5  briefly considers some possible applications and developments of this result.

                         ≿

                         F 
                      -ness by some other kind of mathematical object. We shall use sequences of real numbers. Such a sequence will be referred to as a  vector .

The concept of “order dimension” will play a crucial role in our representation. This notion is defined in terms of “extensions” and “realizers” of partial orders. More precisely, a linear order ( 
                         X 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               ≽ 
                            
                            
                               ∗

                         X 
                      ,  
                         ≽ 
                      ) iff

                            X 
                            , 
                            ≽ 
                            )

                            X

                            )

                         X

                         ≽

                         a 
                         ≽

                         a 
                         
                            
                               ≽

                         X

                         ≽

                         ≽

                         ≽

                         ≽

                         X

                         ≽

                         k

Let us go on to show that any quasi-order of countable dimension can be represented by a vector of reals. 
                         8 
                      
                      
                         8 
                         That this holds for partial orders on finite sets  
                               X 
                             (which orders are hence of finite dimension) is shown by  Trotter (1992, p. 39, Theorem 3.2) . Trotter attributes the result to  Dushnik and Miller (1941) .

It is easy to see that  Theorem 1*  is a substantial strengthening of  Theorem 1 . It may well be the case that the set of  
                         a 
                      ,  
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                      , such that  
                         a 
                         ≿ 
                         b 
                      , is uncountable, although the dimension of ( 
                         
                            X 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                         
                            ≿ 
                         
                         
                      ) is countable. As a simple example, let  
                         X 
                       be the set of real numbers, and let  
                         ≿ 
                       be  
                         ≥ 
                      . Since ( 
                         
                            X 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                         
                            ≿ 
                         
                         
                      ) is in this case a linear order, it is of dimension 1.

Let us recapitulate the standard definition of an extensive structure. Such a structure is of the form ( 
                         X 
                      ,  
                         ≿ 
                      ,  
                         ∘ 
                      ), where  
                         ∘ 
                       is a closed binary operation of concatenation, mapping  
                         X 
                         × 
                         X 
                       into  
                         X 
                      . Thus, let  
                         X 
                       be a non-empty set, let  
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               + 
                            
                         
                       be the set of positive integers, define ∼ and  
                         ≻

Theorems 1 and 2  together suggest the possibility of vector-valued extensive measurement, on structures involving mere quasi-orders. The remainder of this paper will be devoted to constructing such a theory. Let us start by broadening the notion of a linear extension, so as to apply to structures of the form (

                             is not antisymmetric. Hence, by contraposition, if  
                               Q 
                             is antisymmetric,  
                               P 
                             contains no alternating cycle.

                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                     + 
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            . Clearly, this construction yields an alternating cycle. We have now shown that if  
                               Q 
                             is not antisymmetric,  
                               P 
                             contains an alternating cycle. Thus, by contraposition, if  
                               P 
                             contains no alternating cycle,  
                               Q 
                             is antisymmetric.

Having proved (1),  Lemma 1  follows immediately from the observation that  
                               Q 
                               = 
                               
                                  tr 
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∪ 
                                  P 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is antisymmetric iff  
                               P 
                             is a subset of a linear extension of  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∘ 
                                  
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            . 
                               21 
                            
                            
                               21 
                               This proof draws heavily on  Trotter ’s ( 1992 , p. 16) proof of Lemma 6.3. 
                            
                            □

                            If

                            has a realizer satisfying Axiom  3 .

                            , again implying that (

Lemmas 1 and 2  imply that for all

                         ∈

                             is monotone. 
                         We shall now extend this result to triples of  
                               M 
                            -sets, by showing that at least one of the four unions

                                     2

                                     3 
                                  
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                            , and  
                               
                                  
                                     S

                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                               
                             is monotone. Given the result of the last paragraph we can, without loss of generality, shorten the proof by assuming, for each pair  
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                            ,  
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                             of  
                               M 
                            -sets, either that  
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               ∪ 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                             is monotone, or that

By Axiom 6, this contradicts the assumption that  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        a

We shall now extend this result to triples of  
                               M 
                            -sets, by showing that at least one of the four unions

                                     3 
                                  
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                               
                             is monotone. Given the result of the last paragraph we can, without loss of generality, shorten the proof by assuming, for each pair

                             of

                             is monotone. Thus, let us assume that the following unions are monotone:

We can finally state the main result of this section:

Theorem 3 
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ≿ 
                                  , 
                                  ∘ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a quasi-order extensive structure iff  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∘ 
                                  
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            has an extensive realizer.

( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ≿ 
                                  , 
                                  ∘ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a quasi-order extensive structure iff  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     ≿ 
                                  
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     ∘ 
                                  
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            has an extensive realizer.

This theorem follows immediately from  Propositions 1, 2 and 4 .

In order to prove representation and uniqueness theorems for quasi-order extensive structures, we must define elementary arithmetical operations on pairs of vectors of equal length. This is straightforward:

                            ) 
                         
                      . Further, we may, in a derived sense, compare vectors to real numbers, with respect to size. A vector  
                         V 
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  2

                            ) 
                         
                       is at least as great as a real number  
                         y 
                       iff  
                         V

                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            y 
                            , 
                            y 
                            , 
                            … 
                            ) 
                         
                      .

Note that any vector-arithmetical operation, as defined here, is identical to a sequence of corresponding operations on real numbers. Essential properties of the real-number operations, such as the associativity and commutativity of addition and multiplication, are shared by the vector operations. More generally, it is easy to verify that our definitions imply that the set of vectors is a vector space over the set of real numbers. Further, the usual interconnections between the operations are preserved, such as that  
                         V 
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  V

                         =

                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         + 
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                      , that  
                         V 
                         − 
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                       iff  
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         + 
                         
                            
                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         V 
                      , and that  
                         V

                               V

                               ∗

                               V 
                            
                            
                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         
                            
                               V

                               ∗ 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         V 
                       (and

                       contains no zero elements).

Now, this representation theorem readily follows:

Unfortunately, (I) does not imply that only similarity transformations are admissible, if a similarity transformation of a vector-valued function  
                         
                            f 
                         
                       is defined as a function  
                         
                         
                            ϕ

                            )

                            f

                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                      ,  
                         V 
                         > 
                         0 
                      , where  
                         V 
                       is a vector. Consider the following example. Suppose that the function  
                         
                            f

Nevertheless, we shall see that (I) implies the crucial properties of a ratio scale; that is, preservation of ratios and a fixed zero point. Clearly, we may assume the vectors involved to be bounded below and above. If  
                         V 
                       is such a vector, let  
                         
                            inf 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            sup 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            V 
                            ) 
                         
                       denote its infimum and supremum, respectively. Regarding ratios, this proposition holds:

A few words should be said about the meaning of vector ratios. Consider a case involving two-element vectors, and suppose that  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            8 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            1 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      . By  Proposition 5 , this implies that  
                         
                            g 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            g 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            4 
                            ) 
                         
                      , for all functions  
                         
                            g 
                         
                       satisfying (I). Hence,  
                         a 
                       is two to four times as  
                         F 
                       as  
                         b 
                      . This is not to say that there is some real number  
                         x 
                      ,  
                         2 
                         ≤ 
                         x 
                         ≤ 
                         4 
                      , such that  
                         a 
                       is  
                         x 
                       times as  
                         F 
                       as  
                         b 
                      . Rather, the value ratio between  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       is the entire interval [2, 4]. To substantiate this claim, note that if  
                         n 
                         < 
                         2 
                      , then  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         > 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               nb 
                            
                            ) 
                         
                      , if  
                         2 
                         ≤ 
                         n 
                         ≤ 
                         4 
                      , then  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                       #  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               nb 
                            
                            ) 
                         
                      , and if  
                         n 
                         > 
                         4 
                      , then  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               nb 
                            
                            ) 
                         
                         > 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Thus,  
                         a 
                       is  
                         F 
                      -er than  
                         b 
                      , incomparable to  
                         2 
                         b 
                      ,  
                         3 
                         b 
                      , and  
                         4 
                         b 
                      , and less  
                         F 
                       than  
                         5 
                         b 
                      ,  
                         6 
                         b 
                      , etc. Similarly, if  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            1 
                            , 
                            3 
                            ) 
                         
                      , then  
                         a 
                       is two-thirds to twice as  
                         F 
                       as  
                         b 
                      .

Concerning direct, pairwise comparisons of vector ratios, the order of the elements is unimportant. If  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            5 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            c 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            5 
                            , 
                            2 
                            ) 
                         
                      ,  
                         a 
                       is two to five times as  
                         F 
                       as  
                         b 
                      , and also two to five times as  
                         F 
                       as  
                         c 
                      . As  Proposition 5  indicates, moreover, only  
                         
                            inf 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            sup 
                         
                       of a ratio are significant in pairwise comparisons. Suppose that, in a case with three-element vectors,  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            4 
                            , 
                            12 
                            , 
                            25 
                            ) 
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            4 
                            , 
                            5 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            c 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            3 
                            , 
                            5 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Thus,  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            3 
                            , 
                            5 
                            ) 
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            c 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            2 
                            , 
                            4 
                            , 
                            5 
                            ) 
                         
                      . This means that  
                         a 
                       is two to five times as  
                         F 
                       as either of  
                         b 
                       and  
                         c 
                      .

However, the difference between  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         / 
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            c 
                            ) 
                         
                      , as regards the middle element, reflects the fact that, although  
                         b 
                         ∥ 
                         c 
                      ,  
                         b 
                       nevertheless has an “advantage” over  
                         c 
                      , in terms of  
                         F 
                      -ness. Arguably,  
                         b 
                       is  at least as F  as  
                         c 
                      , but not vice versa. (This sense of “at least as  
                         F 
                       as” cannot, of course, be defined as  
                         ≿ 
                      .) For any  
                         d 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                      , if  
                         d 
                         ≻ 
                         b 
                      , then  
                         d 
                         ≻ 
                         c 
                      . But the converse does not hold. If, for instance,  
                         
                            f 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            d 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         
                            ( 
                            3 
                            , 
                            4 
                            , 
                            6 
                            ) 
                         
                      , then  
                         b 
                         ∥ 
                         d 
                      , but  
                         d 
                         ≻ 
                         c 
                      . This asymmetry between  
                         b 
                       and  
                         c 
                       does not show up in the pairwise comparisons between  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                      , or  
                         a 
                       and  
                         c 
                      , but only if we consider all three objects. For vector values with more than three elements things get even more complex, but the general idea should be clear. On reflection, it is evident that structures allowing for incomparability must contain such merely indirect differences.

In the light of  Propositions 5 and 6 , we may call a vector-valued function satisfying (I) a  vector ratio-preserving  scale. Adopting this piece of terminology, our uniqueness theorem can be put as follows:

Theorem 5 
                         
                            A relational structure  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ≿ 
                                  , 
                                  ∘ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a quasi-order extensive structure iff there is a vector-valued function  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                             
                            on  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            , such that  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                             
                            is a vector ratio-preserving scale.

A relational structure  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ≿ 
                                  , 
                                  ∘ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            is a quasi-order extensive structure iff there is a vector-valued function  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                             
                            on  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            , such that  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                             
                            is a vector ratio-preserving scale.

This theorem follows immediately from  Theorem 4 , in conjunction with  Propositions 5 and 6 .

Although the results of this paper are hopefully of some interest in their own right, empirical applications would of course enhance their relevance. Admittedly, it may not be easy to find straightforward applications in the behavioural or social sciences. It is not too difficult to find cases where we can reasonably assume to be dealing with incompletely ordered phenomena. The problem is rather that, in most such cases, not all of the axioms defining a quasi-order extensive structure are likely to be satisfied. Concerning utility or preference measurement, for example, at least Axiom 3* will probably be violated. 
                         22 
                      
                      
                         22 
                         
                            Krantz et al. (1971, p. 124) ;  Roberts (1979, p. 125f) .

Two possible exceptions are the attributes of risk and subjective probability. 
                         23 
                      
                      
                         23 
                         That our results might be applicable to the measurement of subjective probability was suggested by an anonymous referee for this journal. 
                       These are often thought to be amenable to standard extensive measurement. However, the required completeness assumptions may not always hold. 
                         24 
                      
                      
                         24 
                         In the case of risk, this is acknowledged by  Krantz et al. (1971, p. 126) . 
                       Suppose that a subject judges that an event  
                         A 
                       is not more probable than another, dissimilar event  
                         B 
                      , and that  
                         B 
                       is not more probable than  
                         A 
                      . Must she then judge  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       to be equiprobable? On the face of it, it does not seem so. Let  
                         C 
                       be an event that she takes to be only slightly more probable than  
                         A 
                      , and suppose that there is independent evidence that her probability ordering is transitive. If this ordering is also complete, it follows that if she holds  
                         A 
                       and  
                         B 
                       to be equiprobable, she judges  
                         C 
                       as more probable than  
                         B 
                      . But it is not evident that rationality requires her to make the latter judgement.

This sketch of an argument for the existence of incomplete probability orderings is admittedly speculative. The important point is just that if risks or subjective probabilities are sometimes only partially ordered, our theory may be directly applicable to the measurement of these phenomena.

Moreover, our results might have indirect significance, even if direct applications should turn out to be scarce. Extensive structures serve as a basis also for other forms of fundamental measurement, such as difference measurement, conjoint measurement, and the measurement of expected utility. 
                         25 
                      
                      
                         25 
                         
                            Krantz et al. (1971, p. 72) . 
                       It may be possible to show, analogously, that quasi-order extensive structures provide a basis for quasi-order versions of these other kinds of measurement.

This might open up for a multitude of applications. Measurement of preferences is one possible example. Preference orderings are commonly thought to satisfy the axioms defining a difference structure. Arguably, however, preference orderings can be incomplete. 
                         26 
                      
                      
                         26 
                         This is noted by, among many other authors,  Krantz et al. (1971, p. 17) . 
                       One argument for this claim is structurally similar to the argument for incomplete subjective probabilities, outlined above. Suppose that a person is offered a choice between a trip to Rome and a trip to Paris. After carefully weighing the respective pros and cons, he cannot make up his mind as to which offer to choose. He decides that he does not prefer Rome to Paris, and that he does not prefer Paris to Rome. Further, suppose he learns that if he chooses Rome, he is entitled to a small extra benefit, worth a few dollars. He prefers this option, “Rome+”, to the original Rome offer. If he prefers Rome+ to Rome, and is indifferent between Rome and Paris, then he prefers Rome+ to Paris, given that his preference ordering is transitive. However, he will most likely be undecided between Rome+ and Paris, as well. If so, his preference ordering is incomplete. 
                         27 
                      
                      
                         27 
                         This type of example dates back at least to  Restle (1961, p. 62f) . Restle used his example to illustrate a somewhat different point.

Again, the cogency of this argument is not really central to the present issue. It suffices to point out that if preference orderings can be incomplete, for whatever reason, preference measurement has to be based on quasi-order difference structures. Furthermore, it should be stressed that our theory may have other and quite different areas of application, besides those we have here considered.

We have shown that any quasi-order on a countable set can be represented by vectors of real numbers. If such an order is supplemented by a concatenation operator, yielding a relational structure that satisfies axioms similar to the standard axioms for an extensive structure, we obtain a scale that is very similar to a ratio scale. Incomparability is thus compatible with extensive measurement. Finally, we briefly considered some possible applications and developments of this result.

I wish to thank the action editor and two anonymous referees for the  Journal of Mathematical Psychology , for their detailed, penetrating, and helpful comments on an earlier version of this paper. I have also benefited from comments by Tom Trotter, Geoff Brennan, Martin Peterson, and Wlodek Rabinowicz.
